title: Utility Maximization
description: Practice applying the utility maximization rule in this exercise.
topic_path: Utility maximization using marginal utility per dollar spent

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best captures the intuition behind the utility maximization rule?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Each time we buy a good, it gives us more utility. Therefore we should buy as much of a good as possible.
If we pay the same price for two goods, we should just split our budget between those two goods.
If two goods give us the same amount of additional happiness, it doesn't matter which one we buy more of
If we are getting the same amount of happiness for each dollar we spend on two goods, the cost per unit of happiness is the same, so we can't alter our spending to be better off
Because total utility always increases as we consume more of a good, we should buy as much as we can of all goods given our budget.

assessment_items.question_content:
Ecrin has $\$30$ to spend on robotrons and flowbots, which each cost $\$2$. Currently, her marginal utility from one more flowbot would be $40$ and her marginal utility from one more robotron would be $30$.

**Which of the following statements best describes how she should allocate her budget?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It doesn't matter which good she buys because they cost the same.
She should buy more robotrons
She should buy more flowbots.
The marginal utility from flowbots is higher so she should always buy more flowbots.
Because the prices are equivalent, she should buy the same number of flowbots and robotrons.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the definition of the utility maximization rule?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A consumer's utility is maximized when the marginal utility per dollar spent on each good is the same for all goods.
A consumer is behaving optimally when they spend the income on the cheapest goods available.
A consumer is behaving optimally when they buy goods that are all the same price.
A consumer should split their income so that they spend the same amount of money on each good.
A consumer's utility is maximized when she has spent all her available budget on the goods that give her the highest marginal utility

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following equations would represent optimal combination for a utility maximizing consumer deciding how much to buy of three goods, X, Y, and Z?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: $\dfrac{MU_X}{P_X}=\dfrac{MU_Y}{P_Y}=\dfrac{MU_Z}{P_Z}$
$MU_X=MU_Y=MU_Z$
$P_X=P_Y=P_Z$
$MU_X\times P_X = MU_Y\times P_Y = MU_Z\times P_Z$
$\dfrac {\Delta TU_X}{\Delta Q_X} = \dfrac{\Delta TU_Y}{\Delta Q_Y} = \dfrac{\Delta TU_Z}{\Delta P_Z}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**If the marginal benefit of an activity is greater than the marginal cost of an activity, what action should an individual take?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Increase consumption
Increase production
Maintain the same level of consumption
Decrease consumption
Decrease production

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the equation that describes the optimal combination of consumption of two goods, X and Y?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: $\dfrac{MU_X}{P_X} = \dfrac{MU_Y}{P_Y}$
$\dfrac{MU_X}{P_Y}$
$\dfrac{MU_X}{P_Y}=\dfrac{MU_Y}{P_X}$
$\dfrac{TU_X}{P_X}=\dfrac{TU_Y}{P_Y}$
$\dfrac{TU_X}{P_Y}=\dfrac{TU_Y}{P_X}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements about rational decision-making is true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A rational agent ignores sunk costs
A rational consumer’s optimal quantity depends on previously determined fixed benefits
A rational producer bases their optimal quantity on fixed costs
Producers consider sunk costs, but consumers do not
Consumers consider fixed costs, but producers do not

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following does NOT describe decision-making based on marginal analysis?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: An individual comparing the average utility they receive from all units of consumption against the average cost of all units of consumption.
An individual comparing the marginal utility of another cup of coffee against the cost of another cup of coffee
A producer comparing the marginal revenue of hiring another worker against the marginal cost of hiring another worker
A producer comparing the benefit of buying another piece of equipment against the cost of another piece of equipment
An individual comparing the additional happiness they get from another cookie against the cost of consuming an additional cookie

assessment_items.question_content:
Maximus, Eli, and Theodore all are spending their entire budget on candy and movie tickets. Candy costs $\$5$ per bag and movies cost $\$10$ per ticket. Their current consumption and marginal utility of that consumption is given in the table below.

 | $Q_\text{candy}$ | $MU_\text{candy}$|$Q_\text{movies}$ | $MU_\text{movies}$
-|-|-|-|-
Theo | $2$ | $30$ | $10$ | $60$
Maximus | $10$ | $60$ | $5$ | $120$
Eli | $8$ | $5$ | $3$ |$5$

**Which boy should alter his consumption?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Max because he shouldn't eat a higher quantity of candy than the other boys.
Theo and Max should change how much candy and movie tickets they buy because they aren't getting the same marginal utility on each one
Eli should increase his consumption of candy
None of the boys are behaving optimally and all should alter their behavior.
All of the boys are behaving optimally, and should not alter their behavior

assessment_items.question_content:
Sasha is a rational consumer that is behaving optimally when he buys candy and movie tickets. When he buys $9$ movie tickets at $\$10$ each, his marginal utility is $100$ utils per dollar spent.

**If the price of candy is $\$5$, what is the marginal utility he is getting from the last bag of candy he has purchased?**


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Because we know that Sasha is behaving optimally, we know he is following the utility maximization rule:

$\dfrac{MU_{candy}}{P_{candy}}=\dfrac{MU_{movies}}{P_{movies}}$

If we know he is getting $100$ utils per dollar spent on movie tickets, this rule becomes:

$\dfrac{MU_{candy}}{P_{candy}}=100$

If we know he is spending $\$5$ on each bag of candy, this becomes:

$\dfrac{MU_{candy}}{\$5}=100$

Solving for the marginal utility of the last unit of candy:

$\begin{align}\dfrac{MU_{candy}}{\$5}&=100\\
\\
MU_{candy}&=\$5*100\\
\\
MU_{candy}&=500
\end{align}$

choices.content: $500$ utils
$100$ utils
$60$ utils
$90$ utils
$20$ utils

assessment_items.question_content:
Nate is currently spending his entire budget on two goods: food and entertainment. He was willing to pay $\$2$ for the last unit of food he purchased and $\$2{,}000$ for the last unit of entertainment he purchased.

**Which of the following is the best explanation of why he is willing to pay so much more for entertainment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Nate is irrational and doesn't understand that food is a necessity and entertainment is not.
Nate's total utility from entertainment is higher than his total utility from food
Nate's total utility from food is higher than his total utility from entertainment.
Nate's marginal utility from food is higher than his marginal utility of entertainment
Nate's marginal utility from entertainment is higher than his marginal utility of food

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the optimal quantity to consume of a good that is free?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The quantity where total utility from that good is maximized
It cannot be determined without a price
The entire amount of a good that exists
Nothing, since the good is free
Any quantity that a person chooses will be optimal

assessment_items.question_content:
Karna is a rational consumer who is spending his entire income on books and coffee. He currently is allocating his consumption so that the following equation is true:

$\dfrac{MU_c}{\$10}=\dfrac{MU_B}{\$20}$

**If the price of books decreases to $\$5$, which of the following best describes why he will change his purchases of coffee and books?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: He will buy the same amount of coffee because his marginal utility per dollar spent on coffee hasn't changed. He will decrease the number of books he buys because the marginal utility per dollar spent on books is smaller.
As the price of books decreases, his bang per buck he gets for books has gone up. Because the bang per buck he gets on books and coffee are no longer equal he will buy more books.
When he reallocates more of his income to books, the marginal utility of each additional book he buys will increase.
His income has increased, so he will increase his consumption of both books and coffee.
When he reallocates more of his income to coffee, the marginal utility of each additional coffee he buys will increase.

assessment_items.question_content:
Emily is a rational consumer who gets utility from socks and music lessons. Her marginal utility from socks is $50$ utils and the price of socks is $\$5$ per pair. Her marginal utility from music lessons is $600$ utils and the price of music lessons is $\$60$

*Assume socks and music lessons are both normal goods*. 

**If music lessons become free, what will be true about her marginal utility of socks once she reallocates her consumption?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

choices.content: We cannot tell what her marginal utility of the last unit of socks she consumes will be
Her marginal utility of the last unit of socks she buys will be zero
Her marginal utility from the last pair of socks purchased will decrease from the original $50$ utils
Her marginal utility of socks will stay at $50$ utils

assessment_items.question_content:
Javier is spending all of his income on books and coffee. At his current amount of consumption, each \$1 coffee gets him 5 units of utility and each \$10 book yields 50 units of utility.

**Which of the following is true based on his current consumption?**



[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Javier should not change his consumption
Javier should buy more books and fewer coffees
Javier should buy more coffee
If Javier consumes one more book his marginal utility will increase.
Javier can increase his total utility by reallocating his budget, but we cannot determine what he should buy more of based on this information.

assessment_items.question_content:
Annabelle paid a $\$20$ admission fee to get into a carnival and is deciding on the best way to spend her money on ferris wheel rides and corn dogs.

**If she is acting rationally, which of the following does NOT factor into her decision making?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The $\$20$ admission fee
The price of ferris wheel rides
How much money she has left
The additional happiness she gets from one more corn dog
The marginal benefit of one more corn dog

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is true at the optimal quantity, whether it is a quantity produced or a quantity consumed?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Marginal benefit equals marginal cost
Marginal benefit is greater than the marginal cost
Net benefit is minimized
Marginal benefit is minimized
An additional unit of happiness is less than its additional cost

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is a key assumption of rational consumer choice theory?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Consumers maximize total utility
Consumers are satisficers
Consumers maximize profit
Consumers spend their money on the cheapest goods
Consumers leave some of their budget unspent

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of following best describes how a consumer makes an optimal decision?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Choose the optimal combination of goods subject to a budget constraint
Choose the combination of goods with the highest total utility
Choose the combination of goods with the highest marginal utility
Choose the combination of goods that minimizes how much is spent
Choose the combination of goods that gives the highest profit
